At the outset, the most significant risks that could imperil project progress have been identified and corresponding mitigation strategies have been incorporated into the project design.
A primary risk concerns access to reliable and timely data from national stakeholders such as the National Institute of Health, Pakistan Meteorological Department and disaster management authorities. Delays in data sharing approvals, interruptions in data feeds or changes in data formats may affect the continuity of analytics pipelines. To mitigate this risk, the PI will initiate formal data-sharing understandings at the project launch and will work through existing professional links established during the NAaaS project.
A second critical risk relates to the integration of heterogeneous data modalities, including structured surveillance records, meteorological time series and unstructured textual reports. Inconsistent spatial units, reporting intervals and terminology may introduce noise and reduce analytical reliability. This risk will be addressed through systematic temporal and spatial normalisation, controlled vocabularies for disease and climate indicators, and incremental validation of each data source before full integration.
A third risk concerns the technical complexity of deploying and maintaining scalable streaming analytics and knowledge graph infrastructures. Performance bottlenecks, configuration errors and system instability could compromise timely analytics. This risk will be mitigated by building on the team’s proven NAaaS infrastructure and by reusing validated Apache Kafka and Spark configurations. Regular load testing, fault-injection experiments and automated monitoring dashboards will be used to detect and resolve performance issues early. The Co-PIs with expertise in stream processing and resource-aware scheduling will lead the design of performance optimisation strategies, while institutional IT services at NUCES will support network and compute stability.
A fourth major risk is related to the availability, motivation and continuity of suitably skilled graduate students, particularly given that applied climate–health analytics and public-sector data integration may appear less attractive to students who prefer commercially trending technologies. This risk will be addressed through early onboarding of students, alignment of project tasks with thesis and final-year project requirements, and clear publication and career development pathways. The PI and Co-PIs will actively mentor students and ensure that tangible research outputs, software artefacts and co-authored publications are embedded in the work plan to sustain engagement.
To address such uncertainties, the project will adopt a continuous risk identification and review process. The PI will chair quarterly project review meetings involving Co-PIs, student leads and representatives of collaborating institutions where progress, emerging challenges and operational constraints will be systematically discussed. A live project risk register will be maintained, documenting newly identified risks, their potential impact, assigned responsibility and mitigation actions. This register will be reviewed and updated at each quarterly meeting.
Institutional leadership at NUCES Islamabad will play a central enabling role in the implementation of the risk management strategy by facilitating administrative approvals, supporting data sharing arrangements, and providing technical infrastructure continuity.

LIST OF REFERENCES
\[1\] Emmanuel Ezenwanne, Kristie L. Ebi, and Nicholas H. Ogden. Climate change and in fectious diseases: a review of evidence and modelling approaches. Environmental Health, 21(1):1–14, 2022.
\[2\] World Health Organization. Climate change and health. World Health Organization, 2015.
\[3\] Ammar M, Moaaz M, Yue C, Fang Y, Zhang Y, Shen S, Deng F. Emerging arboviral diseases in Pakistan: Epidemiology and public health implications. Viruses. 2025 Feb 7;17(2):232.
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
Pakistan faces an escalating public health risk driven by climate variability, extreme weather events, and rapid environmental degradation. Recurrent floods, heatwaves, droughts and persistent urban smog are increasingly associated with surges in communicable and climate-sensitive diseases, yet the country’s current surveillance and response mechanisms remain largely reactive, fragmented and institutionally siloed. Climate, meteorological, health and media data are generated by multiple agencies and organizations but are not systematically integrated to support timely, evidence-based decision making\[1\].
This project proposes the development of a Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP) as a national level, AI-enabled observatory to bridge Pakistan’s growing climate–health data divide. The platform will integrate heterogeneous data streams—including sub-national meteorological observations, public health surveillance records, and large-scale Urdu and English news and media reports—into a unified multimodal knowledge infrastructure. By moving beyond isolated datasets, CHIP will provide an integrated view of environmental stressors and health outcomes across diverse ecological and socio demographic settings of Pakistan.
The central scientific objective of the project is to establish a unified multimodal intelligence framework capable of modelling the complex, non-linear and context-dependent relationships between climate hazards and population health. The project aims to design automated data ingestion and harmonization pipelines to transform unstructured and structured data into a continuously updated climate–health knowledge graph. Advanced machine learning and temporal graph modelling techniques will be employed to enable early detection of emerging disease risks, particularly where media and community reporting signals precede official surveillance notifications.
PRIORITY AREA OF NATIONAL RELEVANCE, AND IMPACT OF PROPOSED NRPU PROJECT ON THE PRIORITY AREA
In the context of Pakistan, the proposed project directly addresses a national priority area of high relevance, namely climate change adaptation, disaster risk reduction and public health resilience, which are explicitly recognized as strategic priorities in national policies and development agendas. Pakistan is among the countries most vulnerable to climate-induced hazards, including floods, heatwaves, droughts and deteriorating air quality, all of which have demonstrable and growing impacts on communicable diseases, respiratory illnesses, malnutrition and population displacement. The increasing frequency and severity of monsoon-related flooding, urban smog episodes and extreme temperature events have placed unprecedented stress on the national health system and disaster response institutions.
Resolving the scientific and operational challenges targeted by this project will therefore generate substantial societal and institutional benefits. The integration of climate, disaster and public health intelligence into a unified and continuously updated early-warning framework will directly strengthen Pakistan’s national resilience by enabling earlier and more reliable detection of water-borne, vector-borne and climate-sensitive disease risks, particularly during monsoon seasons and extreme weather events. This is of immediate relevance to highly vulnerable population groups, including children, elderly citizens, informal urban settlements and low-income rural communities, who are disproportionately exposed to climate-related health shocks and who often remain outside the reach of conventional surveillance systems\[2\].
From a national governance and service delivery perspective, the proposed platform will provide timely, explainable and spatially precise intelligence to public sector stakeholders, including the National Institute of Health (NIH), National and Provincial Disaster Management Authorities (NDMA and PDMAs) and the Ministry of Climate Change. The availability of district-level and event-driven analytics through operational dashboards will support proactive resource allocation, targeted preventive interventions, early mobilization of emergency response units and improved inter-agency coordination during climate
induced public health emergencies. Importantly, the platform is explicitly designed to integrate with routine institutional workflows and reporting structures, rather than creating a parallel research-only system, thereby enhancing its operational relevance and likelihood of adoption.
The project further contributes to a national priority of strengthening digital governance and data-driven decision making in the public sector. By developing an interoperable, explainable and locally contextualized climate–health intelligence platform, the project will help bridge existing data silos between health, climate and disaster management institutions in Pakistan and will introduce advanced analytical capabilities that are currently unavailable within most public sector information systems.
In addition to its immediate operational impact, the project will contribute to sustainable national capacity building in public-health informatics, climate analytics and applied artificial intelligence. Through close collaboration between academic researchers and public institutions, the project will help develop local expertise in large-scale data integration, natural language processing and knowledge graph–based reasoning for public sector applications. This will establish a national knowledge base in climate–health intelligence and position Pakistan as an emerging regional contributor in data-driven climate resilience and public health surveillance research.
Finally, by enabling earlier intervention, improved preparedness and more targeted risk mitigation strategies, the proposed platform has the potential to reduce the substantial economic burden associated with climate-sensitive diseases and disasters in Pakistan. These include direct healthcare expenditures, productivity losses, disruption of livelihoods and longer-term impacts on human capital. In a country where climate-related disasters have repeatedly resulted in large-scale economic disruption and strain on public finances, strengthening climate–health intelligence represents a strategically important investment towards long-term socio-economic stability and sustainable development.

PROJECT DESCRIPTION
Problem Statement
Pakistan faces a rapidly growing and under-addressed public-health risk arising from the increasing frequency and intensity of climate-related hazards, including heatwaves, floods, extreme rainfall and prolonged periods of poor air quality. These climate stressors are closely linked with surges in climate sensitive diseases such as dengue, malaria, cholera, diarrhoeal illnesses and acute respiratory infections. Although multiple public institutions in Pakistan routinely collect epidemiological, meteorological and disaster-related data, these information streams remain fragmented, delayed and operationally isolated. As a result, national and provincial stakeholders lack a unified, timely and analytically integrated view of emerging climate–health risks at the spatial and temporal resolution required for early intervention\[2\].
Existing public-health surveillance mechanisms in Pakistan are primarily retrospective and report-driven, relying on periodic summaries and manual consolidation of information. They are not designed to systematically integrate real-time climate indicators, disaster events and situational intelligence from unstructured sources such as news and public reports. Consequently, early signals of outbreaks and climate-linked health stress often remain undetected until case numbers have already escalated, limiting the effectiveness of preventive action, targeted resource allocation and coordinated response by health, climate and disaster management authorities.
At the same time, large volumes of potentially valuable situational intelligence are continuously generated through national media and institutional reports, which frequently provide early community-level signals of environmental disruptions, disease clusters and service pressures. However, the absence of scalable natural language processing, geo-temporal normalisation and cross-domain entity linking mechanisms prevents this unstructured information from being systematically transformed into actionable public
health intelligence as shown in Fig. 1. Moreover, current analytical practices do not provide explainable and auditable evidence linking climate stressors, disaster events and health outcomes, which is essential for policy adoption and institutional trust\[3\].

Despite the availability of modern stream-processing frameworks and artificial intelligence techniques, Pakistan currently lacks an operational, end-to-end and institutionally grounded platform that can continuously ingest heterogeneous climate, disaster, epidemiological and media data, harmonise them in real time, model their interdependencies and generate explainable early-warning insights for decision
makers\[4\]. The absence of such an integrated capability represents a critical gap in national preparedness and climate resilience, particularly during monsoon seasons and extreme weather events when vulnerable populations are most at risk\[5\].
The Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP) project therefore addresses the fundamental problem of transforming fragmented, delayed and siloed data streams into a unified, real-time and explainable climate–health intelligence infrastructure for Pakistan. By extending the validated NAaaS backbone with multimodal data integration, domain-specific natural language processing, climate–health knowledge graph construction and predictive risk modelling, the project seeks to enable timely detection of climate
linked health risks and provide operational decision support to public institutions responsible for health, climate and disaster management.
.
**Methodology:**
This project adopts a multi-phase, data-driven and institutionally grounded methodology to develop a Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP) for Pakistan by extending the existing News Analytics as a Service (NAaaS) infrastructure. The proposed approach is designed to support continuous public-health surveillance, early detection of climate-sensitive disease risks and evidence-based decision-making for national stakeholders. The methodology integrates heterogeneous data streams, advanced natural language processing, geospatial analysis and explainable analytics within a unified and scalable system architecture.
System Architecture and Multimodal Data Ingestion
At the core of the proposed platform is a streaming-oriented data architecture built using Apache Kafka and Spark Structured Streaming, which will enable continuous ingestion and harmonisation of epidemiological, climatic, disaster and media data. Epidemiological surveillance data will be obtained from the National Institute of Health (NIH), Pakistan, including weekly IDSR reports and routine notifications of climate-sensitive diseases such as dengue, malaria, cholera and acute respiratory infections as shown in Fig. 2. These health data will be integrated with real-time and historical meteorological observations, including temperature, precipitation and humidity, sourced from the Pakistan Meteorological Department and the Climatological Data Processing Centre. Disaster and hazard information, including floods, heatwaves, droughts , will be incorporated from the National Disaster Management Authority and Provincial Disaster Management Authorities. In parallel, continuous news streams from established national media outlets, such as Dawn and Express Tribune, will be collected to capture early situational signals and community-level reporting that frequently precede formal health notifications. All incoming streams will be consolidated into a unified and schema-controlled data repository to support traceable and reproducible downstream analyses.
To ensure reliable linkage between climate stressors, disaster events and health outcomes, all datasets will undergo systematic temporal and spatial normalisation. Temporal expressions appearing in unstructured sources, including news articles and situation reports, will be extracted and normalised using HeidelTimeSpatial\[6\] references will be resolved to standardised administrative units in combination with map services. This geocoding process will align all observations to district-level boundaries, which represent the most operationally relevant spatial resolution for surveillance and response planning in Pakistan.

**Natural Language Processing and Domain-Specific Feature Engineering**
A dedicated natural language processing layer will be developed to transform unstructured textual information into structured, epidemiologically meaningful features. Transformer-based language models, including BERT and RoBERTa\[7\]\[8\]will be fine-tuned using locally curated corpora derived from NIH surveillance reports and Pakistani news articles to improve contextual understanding of disease terminology, climate descriptors and administrative references. Named entity recognition and relation extraction models will be applied to identify diseases, symptoms, climatic variables, disaster events and geographic locations. In addition, event-centric and causal narrative detection techniques will be employed to capture implicit associations between environmental stressors and health outcomes, such as reporting patterns linking heavy rainfall and flooding with subsequent increases in vector-borne disease cases. The resulting structured representations will form a shared analytical layer for both statistical modelling and knowledge-driven reasoning.
The extracted entities and relationships will be integrated into a dedicated Climate--Health Knowledge Graph (CHKG). The knowledge graph will explicitly encode connections between diseases, climatic indicators, disaster events, locations and time periods, enabling systematic representation of complex and multi-factorial climate--health interactions. Domain-specific relationship types will be defined to represent associations, co-occurrences and potential triggering mechanisms. The CHKG will serve as the foundation for explainable analytics through graph querying and graph-based machine learning, including graph neural network models. Furthermore, a retrieval-augmented generation layer based on graph retrieval will be implemented to produce evidence-backed and auditable summaries for policy stakeholders, ensuring that automatically generated insights remain grounded in verified data sources.
Building upon the harmonised datasets and the knowledge graph, a multi-layer analytical framework will be developed to support descriptive, predictive and inferential analysis. Descriptive and exploratory analysis will be used to visualise spatio-temporal patterns of disease incidence in relation to temperature, rainfall and extreme climate indicators, enabling identification of historical climate--health associations at the district level. For short-term forecasting and early-warning purposes, time-series models, including long short-term memory networks and Prophet, will be applied to predict disease incidence and detect abnormal surges in high-risk districts, particularly during monsoon and heatwave periods\[9\]. To ensure epidemiological validity and interpretability, generalised linear models, will be employed to quantify relative risks associated with temperature and precipitation anomalies. Lag-based exposure models will be incorporated to account for incubation periods and delayed climate effects on disease occurrence.
Integration of Apache-Based Streaming and Distributed Data Processing Framework
The proposed platform will leverage an Apache-based big data processing stack to support scalable data ingestion, stream processing and large-scale analytics across heterogeneous climate, health and media sources. Apache Kafka will be used as the primary data streaming backbone to enable reliable, fault tolerant and near real-time ingestion of epidemiological bulletins, meteorological feeds, disaster reports and continuous news streams. All incoming data will be organized into source-specific and domain-specific topics to support modular processing and independent pipeline evolution.

Apache Spark-based pipelines will be used to execute natural language processing workflows, including text normalization, tokenization, entity recognition and feature engineering, directly over streaming and historical data. This unified streaming–batch processing design enables consistent analytics logic for both real-time situational monitoring and retrospective model training. The integration of Apache Kafka with Spark will further allow scalable orchestration of downstream tasks such as temporal tagging a geocoding, schema alignment and preparation of graph-ready datasets for knowledge graph construction\[10\].
The final operational component of the methodology is the development of a web-based Climate--Health decision support dashboard for institutional stakeholders, including the National Institute of Health, the National Disaster Management Authority and the Ministry of Climate Change. The dashboard will provide interactive district-level visualisations of climate stressors and disease trends, automated alerts for emerging climate-linked health risks and explainable, evidence-grounded summaries generated from the Climate--Health Knowledge Graph. The overall system is designed to integrate with routine public-health surveillance workflows in Pakistan and to support proactive preparedness and response planning, particularly during recurrent monsoon seasons and periods of extreme weather.
**Plans for Dissemination:**
The project will employ several formal mechanisms to communicate findings to government and policy stakeholders. Policy-oriented technical briefs will be produced for NIH, NDMA and the Ministry of Climate Change, summarising emerging climate–health risks, analytical insights and system performance during key periods such as monsoon and heatwave seasons. These briefs will focus on operationally actionable findings rather than academic methodology.
In addition, structured stakeholder workshops and demonstration sessions will be organised at NUCES Islamabad and, where feasible, at partner institutions. These sessions will be used to present dashboard functionalities, early-warning indicators and explainable analytical narratives generated by the Climate– Health Knowledge Graph. Feedback from these engagements will be formally documented and incorporated into subsequent system iterations, thereby supporting both dissemination and system co
development.
The project will also produce a consolidated technical and operational report at the end of the project period. This report will document the system architecture, data integration procedures, analytical models, validation results and recommended institutional deployment practices. The report will be submitted to relevant government partners and will serve as a reference guide for potential scale-up and long-term institutional adoption of the CHIP platform.
Finally, a publicly accessible project webpage hosted by the PCN research group will be maintained to disseminate non-sensitive project updates, software documentation, selected open-source components, and recorded demonstrations. This platform will provide a sustained dissemination channel for researchers, practitioners and policy stakeholders and will contribute to long-term visibility and reuse of the project outcomes beyond the funding period.
Ethical considerations related to the proposed research (If any):
The proposed Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP) project is designed as an applied data integration and analytics initiative that operates primarily on secondary data sources generated by public institutions and publicly available information streams. Nevertheless, the nature of health-related and location-specific data introduces important ethical, legal and societal considerations that must be explicitly addressed to ensure responsible and trustworthy deployment.
A primary ethical concern relates to the handling of health surveillance data obtained from the National Institute of Health (NIH) and related public-sector partners. Although these datasets are expected to be aggregated at district or higher administrative levels and will not include personally identifiable information, there remains a risk of indirect identification when health indicators are combined with fine
grained spatial and temporal attributes. All analytical outputs will be generated at approved administrative resolutions, and access to any restricted datasets will be governed by formal data-sharing agreements with the respective authorities.

With respect to informed consent, the CHIP project does not involve direct recruitment of individual human participants, clinical interventions, or primary data collection from patients or community members. All health, climate and disaster datasets will be accessed through authorised institutional channels in anonymised and aggregated form. Consequently, individual informed consent is not applicable in the conventional sense. Instead, institutional consent and authorisation will be obtained through formal data access agreements and memoranda of understanding with data-providing organisations, including the National Institute of Health, the Pakistan Meteorological Department and the National Disaster Management Authority.

**Deliverables**
• Raw multimodal datasets collected from NIH, PMD, NDMA/PDMA and national media sources and convert them to unified and documented data schema for climate, health and media events.
• Pre-processed textual corpora from news articles and institutional reports followed by en-gineered textual and contextual features for climate events, disease mentions and public health stressors.
• Fine-tuned Named Entity Recognition (NER) models for diseases, climate variables, locations and organisations.
• Populated Climate–Health Knowledge Graph implemented and construct embeddings models and learned node representations.
• Trained predictive models for climate-sensitive disease risk estimation.
• Configured and validated Apache Kafka cluster for real-time ingestion of health, climate and media streams.
• Implemented Kafka producers and topic schemas for NIH, PMD, NDMA/PDMA and news data sources.
• Integrated streaming outputs into the unified project data store and downstream analytics pipeline.
• End-to-end operational prototype of the Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP). • Web-based interactive dashboards for climate indicators, disease trends and early warning alerts.
• System documentation, deployment package and user manuals for institutional adoption.

**PROJECT WEAKNESSES AT LAUNCH**
At the launch of the proposed Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP) project, the research team has critically reviewed the technical, institutional and human-resource readiness of the initiative. Although the team brings strong prior experience through the successful completion of the NRPU-funded NAaaS project and an established research environment within the PCN research group at NUCES Islamabad, a small number of capacity gaps have been identified that must be proactively addressed to ensure timely and high-quality delivery of nationally relevant outcomes.
The first and most significant capacity gap relates to the availability and sustained engagement of suitably motivated PhD and postgraduate research students for a long-horizon, data- and infrastructure-intensive project. In the current academic environment, many high-performing students are attracted primarily towards short-term, commercially popular “buzzword” areas such as generative AI and application-centric development, rather than systems-oriented and public-sector focused research such as climate–health surveillance. This creates a risk of limited initial interest and delayed onboarding of students with the required analytical and software engineering depth. To address this gap, the project will actively embed CHIP activities into formal MS and PhD research tracks within the PCN research group, align student theses directly with funded project deliverables, and offer co-supervision and publication pathways clearly linked to high-impact applied research. Early exposure workshops and structured problem statements will be used to demonstrate the societal relevance, policy impact and career value of climate–health analytics, thereby improving recruitment and long-term student retention for this research agenda.
A second capacity gap concerns limited in-house domain expertise in epidemiology and public-health operations at the project launch stage. While the research team has strong capabilities in artificial intelligence, distributed systems and data analytics, effective modelling of disease surveillance data requires deeper understanding of reporting practices, indicator definitions, outbreak thresholds and public-health workflows used by national institutions such as NIH and NDMA. This gap will be addressed through structured technical engagement with partner institutions, including regular consultative meetings with public-health and disaster-management professionals, joint validation of analytical outputs and iterative refinement of modelling assumptions. Short domain-orientation sessions will be organised for project researchers to ensure correct interpretation of epidemiological indicators and reporting structures. This approach will allow the project to rapidly internalise domain knowledge without requiring permanent in-house medical expertise.
The third identified gap is related to data access stability and institutional interoperability at the initial stage of the project. Although the team has prior experience of institutional data integration through NAaaS, climate, health and disaster data are governed by different custodians, reporting cycles and data sharing policies. Delays in formal approvals, changes in reporting formats and partial data availability could affect early development and validation of analytics pipelines. To mitigate this risk, the project will adopt a staged data integration strategy. During the first phase, publicly available historical datasets and media streams will be used to develop and validate ingestion, NLP and modelling components. In parallel, formal data access agreements will be pursued with NIH, PMD and NDMA. The system architecture will be designed to be schema-flexible and modular, enabling rapid adaptation to revised formats once institutional feeds become available.
**RISK MANAGEMENT STRATEGY**
At the outset, the most significant risks that could imperil project progress have been identified and corresponding mitigation strategies have been incorporated into the project design.
A primary risk concerns access to reliable and timely data from national stakeholders such as the National Institute of Health, Pakistan Meteorological Department and disaster management authorities. Delays in data sharing approvals, interruptions in data feeds or changes in data formats may affect the continuity of analytics pipelines. To mitigate this risk, the PI will initiate formal data-sharing understandings at the project launch and will work through existing professional links established during the NAaaS project.
A second critical risk relates to the integration of heterogeneous data modalities, including structured surveillance records, meteorological time series and unstructured textual reports. Inconsistent spatial units, reporting intervals and terminology may introduce noise and reduce analytical reliability. This risk will be addressed through systematic temporal and spatial normalisation, controlled vocabularies for disease and climate indicators, and incremental validation of each data source before full integration.
A third risk concerns the technical complexity of deploying and maintaining scalable streaming analytics and knowledge graph infrastructures. Performance bottlenecks, configuration errors and system instability could compromise timely analytics. This risk will be mitigated by building on the team’s proven NAaaS infrastructure and by reusing validated Apache Kafka and Spark configurations. Regular load testing, fault-injection experiments and automated monitoring dashboards will be used to detect and resolve performance issues early. The Co-PIs with expertise in stream processing and resource-aware scheduling will lead the design of performance optimisation strategies, while institutional IT services at NUCES will support network and compute stability.
A fourth major risk is related to the availability, motivation and continuity of suitably skilled graduate students, particularly given that applied climate–health analytics and public-sector data integration may appear less attractive to students who prefer commercially trending technologies. This risk will be addressed through early onboarding of students, alignment of project tasks with thesis and final-year project requirements, and clear publication and career development pathways. The PI and Co-PIs will actively mentor students and ensure that tangible research outputs, software artefacts and co-authored publications are embedded in the work plan to sustain engagement.
To address such uncertainties, the project will adopt a continuous risk identification and review process. The PI will chair quarterly project review meetings involving Co-PIs, student leads and representatives of collaborating institutions where progress, emerging challenges and operational constraints will be systematically discussed. A live project risk register will be maintained, documenting newly identified risks, their potential impact, assigned responsibility and mitigation actions. This register will be reviewed and updated at each quarterly meeting.
Institutional leadership at NUCES Islamabad will play a central enabling role in the implementation of the risk management strategy by facilitating administrative approvals, supporting data sharing arrangements, and providing technical infrastructure continuity.

Deliverables 
• Raw multimodal datasets collected from NIH, PMD, NDMA/PDMA and national media sources and convert them to unified and documented data schema for climate, health  and media events.

**PROJECT WEAKNESSES AT LAUNCH** 
At the launch of the proposed Climate–Health Intelligence Platform (CHIP) project, the research team has  critically reviewed the technical, institutional and human-resource readiness of the initiative. Although  the team brings strong prior experience through the successful completion of the NRPU-funded NAaaS  project and an established research environment within the PCN research group at NUCES Islamabad, a  small number of capacity gaps have been identified that must be proactively addressed to ensure timely  and high-quality delivery of nationally relevant outcomes. 
The first and most significant capacity gap relates to the availability and sustained engagement of suitably  motivated PhD and postgraduate research students for a long-horizon, data- and infrastructure-intensive  project. In the current academic environment, many high-performing students are attracted primarily  towards short-term, commercially popular “buzzword” areas such as generative AI and application-centric  development, rather than systems-oriented and public-sector focused research such as climate–health  surveillance. This creates a risk of limited initial interest and delayed onboarding of students with the  required analytical and software engineering depth. To address this gap, the project will actively embed  CHIP activities into formal MS and PhD research tracks within the PCN research group, align student theses  directly with funded project deliverables, and offer co-supervision and publication pathways clearly linked  to high-impact applied research. Early exposure workshops and structured problem statements will be  used to demonstrate the societal relevance, policy impact and career value of climate–health analytics,  thereby improving recruitment and long-term student retention for this research agenda. 
A second capacity gap concerns limited in-house domain expertise in epidemiology and public-health  operations at the project launch stage. While the research team has strong capabilities in artificial  intelligence, distributed systems and data analytics, effective modelling of disease surveillance data  requires deeper understanding of reporting practices, indicator definitions, outbreak thresholds and  public-health workflows used by national institutions such as NIH and NDMA. This gap will be addressed  through structured technical engagement with partner institutions, including regular consultative  meetings with public-health and disaster-management professionals, joint validation of analytical outputs  and iterative refinement of modelling assumptions. Short domain-orientation sessions will be organised  for project researchers to ensure correct interpretation of epidemiological indicators and reporting  structures. This approach will allow the project to rapidly internalise domain knowledge without requiring  permanent in-house medical expertise. 
The third identified gap is related to data access stability and institutional interoperability at the initial  stage of the project. Although the team has prior experience of institutional data integration through  NAaaS, climate, health and disaster data are governed by different custodians, reporting cycles and data sharing policies. Delays in formal approvals, changes in reporting formats and partial data availability  could affect early development and validation of analytics pipelines. To mitigate this risk, the project will  adopt a staged data integration strategy. During the first phase, publicly available historical datasets and  media streams will be used to develop and validate ingestion, NLP and modelling components. In parallel,  formal data access agreements will be pursued with NIH, PMD and NDMA. The system architecture will  be designed to be schema-flexible and modular, enabling rapid adaptation to revised formats once  institutional feeds become available.
**RISK MANAGEMENT STRATEGY** 
At the outset, the most significant risks that could imperil project progress have been identified and  corresponding mitigation strategies have been incorporated into the project design. 
A primary risk concerns access to reliable and timely data from national stakeholders such as the National  Institute of Health, Pakistan Meteorological Department and disaster management authorities. Delays in  data sharing approvals, interruptions in data feeds or changes in data formats may affect the continuity  of analytics pipelines. To mitigate this risk, the PI will initiate formal data-sharing understandings at the  project launch and will work through existing professional links established during the NAaaS project.  
A second critical risk relates to the integration of heterogeneous data modalities, including structured  surveillance records, meteorological time series and unstructured textual reports. Inconsistent spatial  units, reporting intervals and terminology may introduce noise and reduce analytical reliability. This risk  will be addressed through systematic temporal and spatial normalisation, controlled vocabularies for  disease and climate indicators, and incremental validation of each data source before full integration.  
A third risk concerns the technical complexity of deploying and maintaining scalable streaming analytics  and knowledge graph infrastructures. Performance bottlenecks, configuration errors and system  instability could compromise timely analytics. This risk will be mitigated by building on the team’s proven  NAaaS infrastructure and by reusing validated Apache Kafka and Spark configurations. Regular load  testing, fault-injection experiments and automated monitoring dashboards will be used to detect and  resolve performance issues early. The Co-PIs with expertise in stream processing and resource-aware  scheduling will lead the design of performance optimisation strategies, while institutional IT services at  NUCES will support network and compute stability. 
A fourth major risk is related to the availability, motivation and continuity of suitably skilled graduate  students, particularly given that applied climate–health analytics and public-sector data integration may  appear less attractive to students who prefer commercially trending technologies. This risk will be  addressed through early onboarding of students, alignment of project tasks with thesis and final-year  project requirements, and clear publication and career development pathways. The PI and Co-PIs will  actively mentor students and ensure that tangible research outputs, software artefacts and co-authored  publications are embedded in the work plan to sustain engagement. 
To address such uncertainties, the project will adopt a continuous risk identification and review process.  The PI will chair quarterly project review meetings involving Co-PIs, student leads and representatives of  collaborating institutions where progress, emerging challenges and operational constraints will be  systematically discussed. A live project risk register will be maintained, documenting newly identified  risks, their potential impact, assigned responsibility and mitigation actions. This register will be reviewed  and updated at each quarterly meeting. 
Institutional leadership at NUCES Islamabad will play a central enabling role in the implementation of the  risk management strategy by facilitating administrative approvals, supporting data sharing arrangements,  and providing technical infrastructure continuity.
